sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presents with chronic back pain that has been ongoing for 2 years, initially occurring after moving furniture, described as a sharp stabbing pain that radiates down to the right leg and foot, with associated numbness and tingling, especially to the right leg.
He also reports difficulty getting comfortable, particularly when sitting.
He has tried physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past, but with temporary relief.
He mentions weight gain due to decreased activity level.
Past medical history: The patient reports no family members with spine conditions.
He has a 20-year history of being a non-smoker.
Review of systems: No numbness or tingling in the genital or rectal area, no loss of sensation in these areas.
No weakness, loss of control of bladder or bowels.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has midline tenderness at L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
There is decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results: MRI shows disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has symptoms consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy, as evidenced by his symptoms, exam, and MRI results.
Problem list: L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy; chronic lumbar back pain; weight gain due to decreased activity level.
Plan:
I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid into the lumbar spine under fluoroscopy to alleviate inflammation and pain.
I will monitor the patient and reassess as needed, with the goal of limiting injections to no more than 4 times a year.
The procedure is relatively quick and common, and the patient can expect to be out of the office within an hour.
Follow-up: I will schedule a follow-up appointment to reassess the patient's response to the injection.
